items.title: Climate change and social health
items.author: Johanna C. Badcock
items.content: Climate change and social health are deeply interconnected: climate impacts erode social conditions, while social disconnection weakens collective climate action. Strengthening social health is essential for resilience and effective policy.

items.title: To regulate vaping we need pragmatic, evidence-based policy
items.author: Jamie Brown
items.content: Vaping regulation is a global challenge. We discuss evidence on health risks, smoking impacts and policy goals, and advocate for pragmatic, proportionate regulation that reduces smoking harms while minimizing youth uptake and unintended consequences.

items.title: E-cigarettes increase harm and should be discouraged
items.author: Stanton A. Glantz
items.content: Ever since electronic cigarettes (e-cigarettes) were first marketed in 2004, they have been promoted as ‘tobacco harm reduction’ on the basis of the assumption that they are a less toxic way for smokers to consume nicotine. As detailed below, current evidence does not support this. E-cigarettes as actually used can increase harm and use should be discouraged.

items.title: How networking harassment fuels the leaky pipeline
items.author: Shahar Hechtlinger
items.content: The leaky pipeline is not only about hiring and promotion but also what happens in networking spaces where harassment goes unchecked. When careers depend on conferences and collaborations, institutions and research communities must stop leaving people to manage the risk alone, argues Shahar Hechtlinger.

items.title: Remote delivery of STEM and entrepreneurship role models at scale changes college major choice in Ecuador
items.author: Mathis Schulte
items.content: A randomized trial in Ecuadorian high schools showed youth videos of both STEM and entrepreneurship role models. This caused both girls and boys to be less likely to enrol in STEM college majors, with girls choosing business and boys other fields.

items.title: The political polarization of health outcomes in the USA
items.author: Neil A. O’Brian
items.content: Using individual-level medical data and death records, this study finds that conservatives in the USA experienced worse health and higher mortality than liberals during the 2010s. No significant gaps in biomarkers or mortality were present before the 2010s.

items.title: Early-career-researcher-led best practices for social science and behavioural genetics
items.author: Mar Talens
items.content: An emerging market promotes embryo selection for behavioural traits including IQ, but the response of the social and behavioural genetic research community remains muted. We call for unified best practices, updated training and participatory ethics so that the field can strengthen accountability for its present-day impact and prevent future harms.

items.title: Digital CBT reduces mental disorders and boosts access to care in college students
items.content: In a population-based randomized clinical trial across 26 US colleges, we demonstrated that digital cognitive–behavioural therapy (CBT) guided self-help, delivered following universally offered mental health screening, reduced the prevalence of having anxiety, depression and/or an eating disorder through 2 years of follow up and substantially increased service utilization, including by students from marginalized groups.
